dashboard.title: Financial Calculators for Smarter Decisions
dashboard.subtitle: Simple tools designed to help business owners plan, price, forecast, and grow with confidence.

categories.name: Sales Planning
categories.description: Set goals and track revenue targets.

categories.name: Marketing Metrics
categories.description: Measure customer acquisition and lifetime value.

calculators.title: Monthly Sales Goal Calculator
calculators.subtitle: Turn annual goals into monthly targets
calculators.description: Set a yearly revenue goal and instantly discover how much you need to generate each month to stay on track. Perfect for business owners, sales teams, and entrepreneurs who want clear and achievable targets.

seo.title: Monthly Sales Goal Calculator
seo.description: Calculate monthly sales targets needed to achieve your annual revenue goals.

benefits: Break annual goals into monthly targets

results.title: Monthly Sales Goal
results.success_message: This is the average amount you need to generate each month to reach your annual goal.

steps.title: Enter Your Annual Goal
steps.description: Add the total revenue you want your business to generate during the year.

steps.title: Review Your Target
steps.description: The calculator divides your annual goal into 12 monthly targets.

steps.title: Create an Action Plan
steps.description: Use your monthly target to guide sales activities and monitor performance.

items.title: Monthly Sales Goal
items.description: The average amount of revenue you need to generate each month to achieve your annual target.

tips: Review your monthly performance regularly.
Adjust goals if market conditions change.
Track progress weekly rather than monthly.
Use historical sales data when setting annual targets.

faqs.question: How is the monthly goal calculated?
faqs.answer: The calculator divides your annual revenue target by 12 months.

faqs.question: Can I use this for quarterly planning?
faqs.answer: Yes. Once you know your monthly target, you can multiply it by 3 to estimate quarterly goals.

faqs.question: Is this suitable for startups?
faqs.answer: Absolutely. It helps startups create realistic revenue targets and growth plans.

example.title: Example Calculation
example.description: If your annual revenue goal is $2.5 million, you need to generate approximately $208,333 each month to stay on track.

calculators.title: Break-Even & Pricing Calculator
calculators.subtitle: Price products with confidence
calculators.description: Find the minimum price you need to charge to cover costs and start generating profit.

seo.title: Break-Even Calculator
seo.description: Calculate your break-even point and determine the ideal selling price.

steps.title: Enter Your Total Costs
steps.description: Add the total amount you need to cover, including materials, labor, overhead, and any other business expenses.

steps.title: Enter the Number of Units
steps.description: Tell us how many products, services, or units you expect to sell.

steps.title: Set Your Profit Goal
steps.description: Choose the profit percentage you'd like to earn above your costs.

steps.title: Add Additional Scenarios
steps.description: Want to compare different situations? Add multiple entries with different costs, quantities, or profit goals.

steps.title: Review Your Pricing
steps.description: See your break-even price and recommended selling price instantly so you can price with confidence.

items.title: Break-Even Price
items.description: This is the minimum price you need to charge per unit to cover your costs. At this price, you won't make a profit, but you won't lose money either.

items.title: Target Selling Price
items.description: This is the recommended price per unit based on the profit percentage you selected.

items.title: Profit Goal
items.description: The percentage of profit you want to earn above your costs.

items.title: Scenario Comparison
items.description: If you've added multiple entries, compare different pricing strategies side by side to find the best option.

tips: Always know your costs before setting prices.
Don't price based solely on competitors—make sure your pricing supports your profit goals.
Review your pricing regularly as costs change.
Use multiple scenarios to see how different profit margins affect your selling price.
A higher selling price doesn't always mean higher profit if it impacts demand.

faqs.question: What is a break-even price?
faqs.answer: A break-even price is the minimum amount you need to charge per unit to cover all your costs without making a profit or loss.

faqs.question: Why should I add a profit percentage?
faqs.answer: Adding a profit percentage helps determine a selling price that not only covers your costs but also generates income for your business.

faqs.question: Can I compare different pricing scenarios?
faqs.answer: Yes. Add multiple entries with different costs, quantities, or profit goals to compare results side by side.

faqs.question: Should I use the target selling price exactly?
faqs.answer: The target selling price is a recommendation. You may choose to adjust it based on market conditions, customer demand, and competitive pricing.

faqs.question: Who should use this calculator?
faqs.answer: Business owners, freelancers, service providers, product sellers, and anyone who needs to determine profitable pricing.

example.title: Example Calculation
example.description: If your total costs are $5,000, you expect to sell 100 units, and you want a 25% profit margin, your break-even price would be $50 per unit and your recommended selling price would be approximately $62.50 per unit.

calculators.title: Sales Forecast Calculator
calculators.subtitle: Plan future growth
calculators.description: Estimate future sales based on current performance and expected growth trends.

seo.title: Sales Forecast Calculator
seo.description: Forecast future sales and revenue growth using historical business data.

results.title: Sales Forecast Results
results.success_message: Based on your historical sales data, this is the projected revenue for next month.

steps.title: Enter Past Sales Data
steps.description: Add your previous monthly revenue figures in chronological order, from oldest to newest.

steps.title: Include Multiple Months
steps.description: The more historical data you provide, the better the forecast can identify trends.

steps.title: Generate Forecast
steps.description: Click Forecast Sales to calculate your estimated revenue for the next month.

steps.title: Review the Trend Chart
steps.description: Compare historical performance with the projected future trend.

items.title: Forecasted Revenue
items.description: The estimated amount of revenue you may generate next month based on historical trends.

items.title: Trend Projection
items.description: A visual representation showing how your sales have changed over time and where they may be heading.

tips: Use at least 3 months of historical sales data for better accuracy.
Include your most recent sales figures for realistic projections.
Update forecasts monthly as new data becomes available.
Use forecasts as guidance, not guarantees.

faqs.question: How does the forecast work?
faqs.answer: The calculator analyzes historical sales data and identifies trends to estimate future revenue.

faqs.question: How many months should I enter?
faqs.answer: At least 3 months is recommended, but adding more historical data can improve forecasting accuracy.

faqs.question: Can I use this for seasonal businesses?
faqs.answer: Yes, but forecasts work best when enough historical data is available to capture seasonal patterns.

faqs.question: Is the forecast guaranteed?
faqs.answer: No. Forecasts are estimates based on historical trends and should be used as planning tools.

example.title: Example Forecast
example.description: If your recent monthly sales were $100,000, $112,000, and $125,000, the calculator may project approximately $137,500 for the next month based on the growth trend.

calculators.title: What-If Profit Calculator
calculators.subtitle: Test scenarios before making decisions
calculators.description: Analyze how pricing, costs, and sales volume changes could impact profits.

seo.title: What-If Profit Calculator
seo.description: Model different business scenarios and understand their financial impact.

results.title: Scenario Analysis Results
results.success_message: Review how different business decisions impact your revenue and profitability.

steps.title: Enter Your Current Numbers
steps.description: Start with your current monthly revenue and monthly costs. Use real numbers whenever possible for the most accurate results.

steps.title: Adjust Revenue Scenarios
steps.description: Move the revenue slider to see what happens if sales increase or decrease. This helps you understand the impact of growth opportunities and potential slowdowns.

steps.title: Adjust Cost Scenarios
steps.description: Use the cost slider to simulate rising expenses, cost reductions, or operational changes. See how even small changes affect profitability.

steps.title: Review the Results
steps.description: Watch your projected revenue, costs, profit, and profit margin update instantly as you change each scenario.

steps.title: Compare Different Possibilities
steps.description: Try multiple combinations of revenue and cost changes to identify risks, opportunities, and the most profitable path forward.

items.title: Projected Revenue
items.description: The estimated amount of money your business would generate under the selected scenario.

items.title: Projected Costs
items.description: The estimated expenses your business would incur after applying the selected cost changes.

items.title: Net Profit
items.description: The amount remaining after costs are deducted from revenue. Positive numbers indicate profit, while negative numbers indicate a loss.

items.title: Profit Margin
items.description: The percentage of revenue that remains as profit. Higher margins generally indicate a healthier business.

items.title: Scenario Summary
items.description: A quick assessment of your selected scenario, highlighting potential risks, opportunities, and areas for improvement.

tips: Test both best-case and worst-case scenarios before making important business decisions.
A small increase in revenue can often have a larger impact on profit than expected.
Monitor costs regularly, as rising expenses can quickly reduce profitability.
Use this calculator during budgeting and planning sessions to prepare for different outcomes.
Focus on improving profit margin, not just increasing revenue.

faqs.question: What is a what-if analysis?
faqs.answer: A what-if analysis allows you to test different business scenarios and see how changes in revenue or costs could impact profitability.

faqs.question: Who should use this calculator?
faqs.answer: Business owners, entrepreneurs, managers, and anyone responsible for planning revenue, expenses, or growth strategies.

faqs.question: Can this predict the future?
faqs.answer: No. The calculator provides estimates based on the assumptions you enter. Actual results may vary.

faqs.question: Why is my profit negative?
faqs.answer: A negative profit means your projected costs are greater than your projected revenue under the selected scenario.

faqs.question: How can I improve my profit margin?
faqs.answer: You can improve profit margin by increasing revenue, reducing costs, improving efficiency, or adjusting pricing strategies.

example.title: Example Scenario
example.description: Imagine your business currently generates $100,000 per month and spends $65,000 on expenses. If revenue increases by 20% while costs increase by only 5%, your profit grows significantly. This calculator helps you visualize those changes before making real-world decisions.

calculators.title: Marketing Cost Per Customer Calculator
calculators.subtitle: Find out which marketing channels bring customers at the lowest cost.
calculators.description: Compare advertising channels, track customer acquisition costs, and discover where your marketing budget delivers the best results.

seo.title: Customer Value Calculator
seo.description: Calculate customer lifetime value and compare it to acquisition costs.

steps.title: Add a Marketing Channel
steps.description: Enter the name of a marketing source such as Facebook Ads, Google Ads, Referrals, Email Marketing, or Events.

steps.title: Enter Your Marketing Spend
steps.description: Add the total amount spent on that marketing channel.

steps.title: Enter Customers Acquired
steps.description: Enter the number of customers generated from that marketing effort.

steps.title: Add Additional Channels
steps.description: Compare multiple marketing channels side by side.

steps.title: Review Your Results
steps.description: See which channels generate customers at the lowest cost and where your marketing budget performs best.

items.title: Cost Per Customer
items.description: The average amount spent to acquire one customer from a specific marketing channel.

items.title: Best Performing Channel
items.description: The marketing source that generates customers at the lowest cost.

items.title: Total Marketing Spend
items.description: The combined amount invested across all marketing channels.

items.title: Total Customers Acquired
items.description: The total number of customers generated from all marketing activities.

tips: Most healthy businesses target an LTV:CAC ratio of at least 3:1.
Focus on increasing customer retention to improve lifetime value.
Reducing acquisition costs can significantly improve profitability.
Review this metric regularly as marketing costs and customer behavior change.
A strong ratio often indicates that you can safely invest more in growth.

faqs.question: What is Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC)?
faqs.answer: CAC measures the average amount spent to acquire one new customer through marketing and sales efforts.

faqs.question: What is Customer Lifetime Value (LTV)?
faqs.answer: LTV estimates the total revenue a customer generates throughout their relationship with your business.

faqs.question: What is a good LTV:CAC ratio?
faqs.answer: Many businesses aim for a ratio of 3:1 or higher. This means customers generate at least three times more value than the cost to acquire them.

faqs.question: What if my ratio is below 1:1?
faqs.answer: A ratio below 1:1 means you're spending more to acquire customers than they're generating in value, which may not be sustainable long term.

faqs.question: Can a very high ratio be a problem?
faqs.answer: Sometimes. If your ratio is extremely high, it could indicate that you're under-investing in marketing and missing growth opportunities.

example.title: Example Calculation
example.description: Imagine you spend $10,000 on marketing and acquire 250 customers. Your acquisition cost is $40 per customer. If each customer generates $50 per month and stays for 12 months, their lifetime value is $600. This creates an LTV:CAC ratio of 15:1, indicating a highly profitable customer acquisition strategy.
